concepts.name: Transformer
concepts.definition: A neural network architecture introduced in 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017) that relies entirely on self-attention mechanisms to process sequences. It replaced recurrent networks and became the backbone of modern large language models.
source_references: Vaswani et al. 2017 - Attention Is All You Need

concepts.name: Attention Mechanism
concepts.definition: A technique that allows a model to dynamically weight the importance of different parts of the input when producing an output. Self-attention lets each token in a sequence attend to all other tokens, capturing long-range dependencies.

concepts.name: Large Language Model
concepts.definition: A neural network with billions of parameters trained on massive text corpora to understand and generate human language. LLMs learn statistical patterns across language and can perform a wide range of tasks through prompting.

concepts.name: Tokenization
concepts.definition: The process of converting raw text into tokens — discrete units (words, subwords, or characters) that a model can process. Tokenizers like BPE and WordPiece build vocabularies that balance coverage and efficiency.
source_references: Sennrich et al. 2016 - BPE for NMT

concepts.name: Embedding
concepts.definition: A dense vector representation of text, images, or other data in a continuous high-dimensional space. Semantically similar items are placed close together, enabling similarity search and downstream ML tasks.

concepts.name: BERT
concepts.definition: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers — a Transformer model pre-trained with masked language modeling and next-sentence prediction. BERT excels at understanding tasks like classification, NER, and Q&A.

concepts.name: GPT
concepts.definition: Generative Pre-trained Transformer — a family of autoregressive language models trained to predict the next token. GPT models (GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4) are the foundation of modern conversational AI and text generation.

concepts.name: Context Window
concepts.definition: The maximum number of tokens an LLM can process at once — both input and output combined. Larger context windows allow longer documents, conversation histories, and more complex prompts.

concepts.name: Token
concepts.definition: The basic unit of text that LLMs process. A token is roughly 4 characters or 0.75 words in English. Models have token limits for both input and output, which determines how much text they can handle at once.

concepts.name: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
concepts.definition: An architecture that enhances LLM responses by first retrieving relevant documents from an external knowledge base, then conditioning the LLM's generation on those retrieved documents. RAG reduces hallucinations and keeps knowledge up-to-date without retraining.
source_references: Lewis et al. 2020 - Retrieval-Augmented Generation for NLP

concepts.name: GraphRAG
concepts.definition: A variant of RAG that uses a knowledge graph as its primary retrieval structure. Instead of fetching similar text chunks, GraphRAG traverses concept relationships to find contextually relevant information, enabling multi-hop reasoning and structured knowledge retrieval.
source_references: Edge et al. 2024 - From Local to Global: A GraphRAG Approach

concepts.name: Semantic Search
concepts.definition: Search that understands the meaning and intent of a query, rather than matching exact keywords. Uses embeddings to find results that are semantically similar even if they use different words.

concepts.name: Dense Retrieval
concepts.definition: A retrieval approach where both queries and documents are encoded into dense vectors using neural networks (bi-encoders), and similarity is computed via dot product or cosine distance. More semantically aware than sparse retrieval.

concepts.name: Sparse Retrieval
concepts.definition: A retrieval approach based on exact or weighted keyword matching using inverted indices. Algorithms like BM25 rank documents by term frequency and inverse document frequency. Fast and interpretable but misses semantic similarity.

concepts.name: BM25
concepts.definition: Best Match 25 — a probabilistic ranking function used in sparse retrieval. It improves on TF-IDF by incorporating document length normalization and term saturation, making it the gold standard for keyword-based retrieval.

concepts.name: Hybrid Search
concepts.definition: A retrieval strategy that combines dense (semantic) and sparse (keyword) retrieval to get the best of both worlds. Results from both methods are merged using reciprocal rank fusion or weighted combination.

concepts.name: HyDE
concepts.definition: Hypothetical Document Embeddings — a technique where the LLM first generates a hypothetical answer to the query, then retrieves documents similar to that hypothetical answer rather than the original query. Improves retrieval for abstract or complex questions.
source_references: Gao et al. 2022 - Precise Zero-Shot Dense Retrieval without Relevance Labels

concepts.name: Multi-hop Reasoning
concepts.definition: The ability to answer questions that require chaining multiple retrieval steps or reasoning steps across different pieces of information. For example, 'Who is the CEO of the company that makes GPT-4?' requires two hops.

concepts.name: Reranker
concepts.definition: A model (typically a cross-encoder) that takes a query and a retrieved document and produces a relevance score. Used as a second-stage after initial retrieval to reorder candidates by true relevance, significantly improving precision.

concepts.name: Chunking
concepts.definition: The process of splitting long documents into smaller, overlapping pieces (chunks) before indexing them in a vector store. Chunk size and overlap are critical hyperparameters that affect retrieval quality.

concepts.name: Document Loader
concepts.definition: A component that reads documents from various sources (PDF, web pages, databases, APIs) and converts them into a standard format for downstream processing in a RAG pipeline.

concepts.name: Vector Database
concepts.definition: A database optimized for storing and querying high-dimensional embedding vectors. Supports approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search to find the most similar vectors to a query in milliseconds.

concepts.name: FAISS
concepts.definition: Facebook AI Similarity Search — an open-source library for efficient similarity search and clustering of dense vectors. Supports billion-scale ANN search with various index types (Flat, IVF, HNSW).
source_references: Johnson et al. 2019 - Billion-scale similarity search with GPUs

concepts.name: ChromaDB
concepts.definition: An open-source, developer-friendly vector database designed for AI applications. Supports local in-memory use and persistent storage, with a simple Python API and metadata filtering.

concepts.name: Pinecone
concepts.definition: A managed, cloud-native vector database offering serverless scaling, metadata filtering, and hybrid search. No infrastructure management required — ideal for production RAG deployments.

concepts.name: Embedding Model
concepts.definition: A neural network that converts text (or other data) into fixed-size dense vectors. The quality of the embedding model directly determines how well semantic search and retrieval will work.

concepts.name: SentenceTransformers
concepts.definition: A Python library that provides easy-to-use, pre-trained transformer models optimized for generating sentence and document embeddings. Based on BERT/RoBERTa and fine-tuned with contrastive learning.

concepts.name: Fine-tuning
concepts.definition: Adapting a pre-trained model on a domain-specific dataset by continuing gradient-based training. Fine-tuning updates all (or most) model weights, making it expensive but highly effective for specialized tasks.

concepts.name: LoRA
concepts.definition: Low-Rank Adaptation — a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that freezes the original model weights and injects small trainable rank-decomposition matrices into each layer. Reduces trainable parameters by 10,000x while matching full fine-tuning quality.
source_references: Hu et al. 2021 - LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models

concepts.name: QLoRA
concepts.definition: Quantized LoRA — combines 4-bit quantization of the base model with LoRA adapters. Enables fine-tuning of 65B+ parameter models on a single consumer GPU by drastically reducing memory requirements.
source_references: Dettmers et al. 2023 - QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs

concepts.name: PEFT
concepts.definition: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning — a HuggingFace library implementing methods (LoRA, Prefix Tuning, Prompt Tuning, IA3) that fine-tune only a small fraction of model parameters, reducing compute and storage costs.

concepts.name: RLHF
concepts.definition: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — a training paradigm where human raters compare model outputs, a reward model is trained on those preferences, and the LLM is fine-tuned using PPO to maximize the reward. Used to align GPT-4 and Claude.

concepts.name: Instruction Tuning
concepts.definition: Fine-tuning a pre-trained LLM on (instruction, response) pairs to teach it to follow natural language instructions. This transforms a base completion model into an assistant that answers questions and follows directions.
source_references: Wei et al. 2021 - Finetuned Language Models are Zero-Shot Learners

concepts.name: Few-shot Learning
concepts.definition: A prompting technique where you provide a small number of input-output examples in the prompt to demonstrate the desired task format and style to the LLM, without updating model weights.

concepts.name: Zero-shot Learning
concepts.definition: Asking an LLM to perform a task without any examples in the prompt, relying entirely on the model's pre-trained knowledge and instruction-following capability.

concepts.name: In-context Learning
concepts.definition: The ability of LLMs to learn new tasks or behaviors from examples provided within the prompt, without gradient updates. Includes zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting as special cases.

concepts.name: Agent
concepts.definition: An LLM-powered system that can autonomously plan and execute multi-step tasks by using tools (web search, code execution, APIs) and making decisions in a loop until the task is complete.

concepts.name: Tool Use
concepts.definition: The capability of an LLM to call external functions, APIs, or tools as part of its reasoning. The model decides when to call a tool, what arguments to pass, and incorporates the result into its answer.

concepts.name: ReAct
concepts.definition: Reasoning + Acting — a prompting framework where the LLM alternates between generating reasoning traces (Thought) and taking tool actions (Act), then observing results (Observe) in a loop. Improves reliability and interpretability of agents.
source_references: Yao et al. 2022 - ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in LLMs

concepts.name: Chain-of-Thought
concepts.definition: A prompting technique that encourages LLMs to generate intermediate reasoning steps before answering. By 'thinking out loud', models perform significantly better on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks.
source_references: Wei et al. 2022 - Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in LLMs

concepts.name: Prompt Engineering
concepts.definition: The practice of designing and optimizing text prompts to elicit desired behaviors from LLMs. Includes techniques like zero-shot prompting, few-shot examples, chain-of-thought, role-playing, and structured output formatting.

concepts.name: Hallucination
concepts.definition: When an LLM generates text that sounds plausible but is factually incorrect or entirely fabricated. A major challenge in LLM deployment, particularly for high-stakes domains.

concepts.name: Grounding
concepts.definition: Connecting LLM outputs to verifiable, real-world information sources. RAG is the primary technique for grounding — the LLM's answer is anchored to retrieved documents, reducing hallucination.

concepts.name: LangChain
concepts.definition: An open-source Python (and JS) framework for building applications with LLMs. Provides abstractions for chains, agents, tools, memory, and document loaders, making it easy to compose LLM-powered applications.

concepts.name: LlamaIndex
concepts.definition: A data framework for connecting LLMs to external data sources. Specializes in indexing, retrieval, and querying over structured and unstructured data, with advanced RAG features like query routing and sub-question decomposition.

concepts.name: Ollama
concepts.definition: A tool for running open-source LLMs (Llama, Mistral, Gemma, etc.) locally on your machine. Provides a simple CLI and REST API, making local inference accessible without cloud dependencies.

concepts.name: Neo4j
concepts.definition: The world's leading graph database, using a native graph storage engine with a Cypher query language. Ideal for knowledge graphs, recommendation engines, and fraud detection where relationships are first-class citizens.

concepts.name: Knowledge Graph
concepts.definition: A structured representation of knowledge as a network of entities (nodes) and relationships (edges). Knowledge graphs encode semantic relationships between concepts, enabling structured reasoning and multi-hop query answering.

concepts.name: Faithfulness
concepts.definition: A RAG evaluation metric measuring whether the generated answer is factually consistent with the retrieved context. High faithfulness means the LLM didn't add information beyond what was retrieved.

concepts.name: RAGAS
concepts.definition: RAG Assessment — an evaluation framework for measuring RAG pipeline quality across multiple dimensions: faithfulness, answer relevance, context precision, and context recall. Uses LLM-as-judge approach.
source_references: Es et al. 2023 - RAGAS: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation

concepts.name: Cross-encoder
concepts.definition: A model architecture that jointly encodes a query and a document together (as a single input) to produce a relevance score. More accurate than bi-encoders but slower — ideal as a reranker in a two-stage retrieval pipeline.

concepts.name: Bi-encoder
concepts.definition: A model architecture that encodes query and document independently into vectors, then computes similarity via dot product or cosine distance. Fast for large-scale retrieval but less precise than cross-encoders.

concepts.name: KV Cache
concepts.definition: A technique where key-value attention matrices from previous tokens are cached during generation, avoiding redundant computation. Enables efficient autoregressive generation but requires significant GPU memory.
